template: {CHARACTER_0} studied a plant diagram showing parts W (leaf), X (fruit), Y (stem), Z (root). In which parts of the plant are the water-carrying and food-carrying tubes found?
options: water: Z only / food: W only
water: Y and Z only / food: W and X only
water: W, Y and Z only / food: W, X and Y only
water: W, X, Y and Z / food: W, X, Y and Z
correct_answer: water: W, X, Y and Z / food: W, X, Y and Z
explanation: Both water-carrying and food-carrying tubes are found in all parts of the plant — leaves (W), fruits (X), stems (Y), and roots (Z). This ensures every part gets the nutrients and water it needs.

template: {CHARACTER_0} wanted to find out if plants take in water through their roots. Two set-ups were prepared — one with roots and one without. Which set-ups form the correct fair test?
options: Set-up 1: plant with roots in coloured water vs. plant without roots in plain water
Set-up 2: two containers with same amount of coloured water, one plant with roots, one without
Set-up 3: plant with roots in coloured water vs. no plant in coloured water
Set-up 4: two plants both with roots, different amounts of water
correct_answer: Set-up 2: two containers with same amount of coloured water, one plant with roots, one without
explanation: To test if roots take in water, one set-up needs roots and one without, keeping water amount and type the same. Set-up 2 uses identical containers with the same amount of blue water for a fair comparison.

template: {CHARACTER_0} studied the life cycle of a mosquito. Oil was sprayed onto possible breeding grounds (water surfaces). In which two stages does this method help to reduce mosquito numbers? (The diagram labels are A, B, C, D representing stages in the life cycle.)
explanation: The larva and pupa stages of the mosquito live in water. Spraying oil on water surfaces reduces breeding grounds for these water-living stages, hence reducing mosquito numbers.

template: {CHARACTER_0} compared a mealworm (larva of a beetle) and a caterpillar (larva of a butterfly). How are they similar at this larva stage of their life cycle?
options: Both do not look like their adults.
correct_answer: Both do not look like their adults.
explanation: Mealworms and caterpillars are both in the larva stage, where they look completely different from their adult forms. At this stage, both eat a lot to grow before turning into a pupa.

diagram:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 380 170" width="380" font-family="Arial,sans-serif">
  <text x="190" y="16" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#1e293b">Flower Pollination Experiment</text>
  <rect x="20" y="35" width="155" height="100" fill="#fef3c7" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="2" rx="6"/>
  <text x="98" y="52" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#92400e">Flower A</text>
  <text x="98" y="67" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#92400e">Part P removed (stamen)</text>
  <text x="98" y="82" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#475569">+ Bag placed over</text>
  <text x="98" y="97" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#dc2626">Result: No seed formed</text>
  <text x="98" y="112" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#64748b">No pollen → no fertilisation</text>
  <rect x="205" y="35" width="155" height="100" fill="#f0fdf4" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="2" rx="6"/>
  <text x="283" y="52" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#15803d">Flower B</text>
  <text x="283" y="67" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#15803d">Part Q removed (petal)</text>
  <text x="283" y="82" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#475569">+ Bag placed over</text>
  <text x="283" y="97" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#15803d">Result: Seed formed ✓</text>
  <text x="283" y="112" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#64748b">Stamen intact → fertilisation</text>
  <text x="190" y="150" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">Conclusion: R (stigma) and S (ovary) are essential for seed formation</text>
  <text x="190" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">Answer: R and S</text>
</svg>
template: {CHARACTER_0} conducted an experiment using two flowers A and B from the same plant. A part was removed from each. Flower A produced fruit; Flower B did not. The flower parts are labeled P (petal), Q (anther), R (stigma), S (ovary). Which parts were removed?
options: Flower A: P removed / Flower B: R removed
Flower A: R removed / Flower B: S removed
Flower A: Q removed / Flower B: P removed
Flower A: Q removed / Flower B: S removed
correct_answer: Flower A: R removed / Flower B: S removed
explanation: Flower B could not form a fruit because part S (the ovary) was removed — the fruit develops from the ovary. Flower A could still form a fruit because only part R (the anther) was removed; it could still receive pollen from another flower.

template: {CHARACTER_0} investigated germination conditions using three set-ups J, K and L with the same amount of cotton wool and number of green beans. J: damp cotton wool, 29°C, with light → germinated. K: dry cotton wool, 29°C, with light → did not germinate. L: damp cotton wool, 0°C, without light → did not germinate. Which observation-conclusion pair is correct?
options: Green beans in J germinated → water and warmth needed
Green beans in J germinated → water and light needed
Green beans in K did not germinate → light not needed
Green beans in L did not germinate → water and warmth not needed
correct_answer: Green beans in J germinated → water and warmth needed
explanation: Set-up J germinated because it had water, oxygen, and warmth (29°C). Sets K (dry) and L (cold) did not germinate. This proves seeds need water and warmth, but not necessarily light, to germinate.

template: {CHARACTER_0} studied a leaf with green and white areas. Some parts were covered with black paper. The plant was then placed in the sun. After several hours, the leaf was tested for food. Which parts A, B and C will most likely have food? (A = white area uncovered; B = green area uncovered; C = green area covered by black paper)
explanation: Part B is green and uncovered, so it had both chlorophyll and sunlight to make food. Part A is white (no chlorophyll) and Part C was covered by black paper (no light), so they could not photosynthesise.

template: {CHARACTER_0} studied examples of behavioural and structural adaptations. Which row shows the correct pairing?
options: presence of humps (behavioural) / longer large intestine (structural)
large colourful feathers (structural) / dancing to attract mates (structural)
inflate to appear larger when threatened (behavioural) / presence of spines (structural)
correct_answer: inflate to appear larger when threatened (behavioural) / presence of spines (structural)
explanation: Inflating its body is a behavioural adaptation (something the animal does), while having spines is a structural adaptation (a physical body part). Both help the animal protect itself from predators.

template: {CHARACTER_0} counted organisms in the school garden: 15 flowering plants, 5 non-flowering plants, 10 animals with no legs, 5 animals with two legs, 20 animals with six legs. Which conclusion(s) could be drawn? A: There were 20 insects. B: There were 20 populations of plants. C: The animals made up one garden community.
explanation: The only certain conclusion is that there were 20 insects because all insects have exactly six legs. We cannot tell the number of populations or if they form one community just from total organism counts.

template: {CHARACTER_0} studied a food web. Statements: A: S is a producer. B: Q and U feed on plants only. C: V and T are both predator and prey. Which statement(s) is/are correct?
explanation: In the food web, V and T are both predator and prey because they eat other organisms and are also eaten. S is not a producer because arrows point toward it, meaning it is an animal that eats others.

template: {CHARACTER_0} studied a diagram grouping materials. Group A includes eraser and balloon; Group B includes mirror and window; Group C includes wire and keys. Which correctly shows the headings A, B and C?
explanation: Group A (eraser, balloon) are made of rubber, Group B (mirror, window) are made of glass, and Group C (wire, keys) are made of metal. These are grouped based on common physical properties.

template: {CHARACTER_0} set up circuits A, B and C, each with a steel rod, a glass rod and a wooden rod, with identical batteries and bulbs. In which circuit(s) will the bulb light up?
explanation: Only Circuit A will light up because the steel rod is a conductor of electricity. The glass and wooden rods in circuits B and C are insulators that block the flow of electricity.

template: {CHARACTER_0} studied four circuits and asked which one will make bulb L light up most brightly. The circuits have different numbers of batteries and bulbs connected in different ways.
options: Circuit with 2 batteries in series and 2 bulbs
Circuit with 3 batteries in series and 1 bulb
Circuit with 1 battery and 3 bulbs in parallel
correct_answer: Circuit with 3 batteries in series and 1 bulb
explanation: The circuit with three batteries in series provides the most electrical energy to the single bulb. More batteries in series give a brighter bulb.

template: {CHARACTER_0} set up a sealed container holding air and water. 100 cm³ of water was removed through a tap, then 300 cm³ of air was pumped in. Which statement is correct at the end of the experiment?
options: The volume of air was 500 cm³.
The volume of water was 400 cm³.
The mass of air was the same as the mass of water.
The air occupied the same amount of space as water.
correct_answer: The volume of air was 500 cm³.
explanation: Air is a gas with no definite volume, so it expands to fill all available space not occupied by water. When 100 cm³ of water was removed, the air filled that space plus the 300 cm³ pumped in, giving 500 cm³ total.

diagram:

template: {CHARACTER_0} wanted to find out how the volume of substance X relates to its mass. Which actions should be taken to ensure accuracy and reliability? A: Repeat the experiment two more times. B: Repeat with different substances. C: Measure volume with a measuring cylinder. D: Keep temperature of substance X constant.
explanation: To make results reliable, the experiment must be repeated (A) and accurate tools like a measuring cylinder must be used (C). Keeping temperature constant (D) ensures a fair test as heat can change volume.

template: {CHARACTER_0} placed a solid made of three substances M (melts at 65°C), N (melts at 58°C, volume 20 cm³) and P (melts at 115°C) into a set-up. What is the most likely volume of liquid collected in the dish when heated to 60°C?
explanation: At 60°C, substance N melts (melting point 58°C). However, N is encased in substance M (melting point 65°C), which remains solid at 60°C. So the liquid N cannot flow out into the dish.

template: {CHARACTER_0} wanted to collect water from seawater using a set-up placed under the sun. What changes could be made to collect more water in the same time? A: Use a wider cup. B: Add more seawater. C: Use a metal container. D: Add ice cubes on the plastic sheet.
explanation: Using a metal container (C) helps seawater absorb heat faster for faster evaporation. Adding ice (D) helps vapour condense more quickly. These changes speed up both evaporation and condensation.

template: {CHARACTER_0} placed a lit candle and a bear behind a wooden screen. A person cannot see the bear. Which statement explains why?
options: No light is entering the person's eyes.
The bear is not reflecting any light.
Only some light can pass through the screen.
No light is reflected from the bear into the person's eyes.
correct_answer: No light is reflected from the bear into the person's eyes.
explanation: The wooden screen is opaque and blocks the light reflected from the bear from reaching the person's eyes. Since light travels in straight lines, nothing hidden behind the solid screen can be seen.

template: {CHARACTER_0} set up an experiment in a dark room with a light sensor reading 30 units. As one object was moved, the reading slowly increased to 40 units then suddenly dropped to 0 units. Which object was moved and in which direction?
options: Move the book towards the lamp.
Move the lamp towards the screen.
Move the screen towards the book.
Move the lamp away from the book.
correct_answer: Move the lamp towards the screen.
explanation: Moving the lamp closer to the screen increased light intensity (30 to 40 units). When the lamp moved too near to the opaque book, the book blocked the light and the sensor reading dropped to 0.

template: {CHARACTER_0} identified sources of energy from a list. Which of the following are all sources of energy? A: sun; B: wind; C: fossil fuels; D: running water
explanation: All options — the Sun, wind, fossil fuels, and running water — are sources of energy that can be converted into other forms like heat or electricity.

template: {CHARACTER_0} studied a balloon-powered toy car inflated before release at point A. It moved to point B then stopped at point C. Which statement is NOT correct?
options: The car continued moving at point B because it still had kinetic energy.
The car stopped at point C because all kinetic energy was converted to other forms.
The elastic potential energy of the balloon was converted to kinetic energy of escaped air at point A.
The car started moving at point A because its potential energy was converted to kinetic energy.
correct_answer: The car started moving at point A because its potential energy was converted to kinetic energy.
explanation: Statement 4 is incorrect because the car's energy at the start was elastic potential energy from the balloon, not the car's own 'potential energy'. This energy was converted into kinetic energy of the air and the car.

template: {CHARACTER_0} swung a bat to hit a ball. Which events are most likely to happen after the ball is hit? A: The ball changes speed. B: The ball changes direction. C: The ball stops immediately. D: The ball decreases in mass.
explanation: When the bat hits the ball, the force causes the ball to change both its speed (A) and direction (B). A force cannot change mass (D), and the ball does not stop immediately due to inertia (C is wrong).

template: {CHARACTER_0} threw a ball downwards to hit a net. After hitting the net, the ball bounced upwards. What force caused the ball to bounce back?
explanation: The net is elastic, so when the ball hits it, the net pushes back with an elastic spring force. This pushing force from the net causes the ball to bounce upwards in the opposite direction.

template: {CHARACTER_0} placed a steel block, wooden block, and button magnet each on an electronic scale and brought a bar magnet near each. Scale readings: steel block decreased (14→12g), wooden block unchanged (14→14g), button magnet increased (14→16g). Which conclusions can be made? A: A force is pulling the steel block upwards. B: The bar magnet attracted the button magnet. C: The bar magnet has no effect on the wooden block.
explanation: The scale reading for the steel block decreased, meaning the magnet was pulling it upward (A). The wooden block reading stayed the same because wood is a non-magnetic material (C). We cannot conclude B from the data alone.

template: {CHARACTER_0} studied water quality in rivers measured by aquatic animals' tolerance to pollutants. River W: P=6, Q=10, S=10, T=8; River X: P=10, Q=12, S=4, T=4; River Y: P=0, Q=4, S=8, T=12; River Z: P=0, Q=0, S=10, T=16. (P,Q have low pollutant tolerance; S,T have high tolerance.) Rank rivers from least to most polluted.
explanation: River X is least polluted — it has the most sensitive animals (P and Q). River Z is most polluted — it only has animals that survive in very dirty water (S and T). Order from least to most: X, W, Y, Z.

  <text x="150" y="165" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">Mist = tiny liquid water droplets</text>

template: {CHARACTER_0} observed white mist near the spout of a boiling kettle. Which correctly describes the white mist and the process by which it was formed?
options: boiling / it does not have a definite shape
boiling / it does not have a definite volume
condensation / it does not have a definite shape
condensation / it does not have a definite volume
correct_answer: condensation / it does not have a definite volume
explanation: The white mist forms when hot steam from the kettle loses heat and condenses into tiny liquid water droplets (condensation). Liquid has a definite volume but no definite shape.

explanation: Part G must have high strength to support the heavy weight of the plant and its pot without breaking.

template: {CHARACTER_0} placed a balloon filled with air on water and observed it floating. When the balloon was pushed downwards into the container, water overflowed. Which describes the air in the balloon? A: The air has mass. B: The air occupies space. C: The air has fixed shape. D: The air can be compressed.
explanation: When pushed down, the balloon displaces water, proving air occupies space (B). The fact that air stayed compressed inside the balloon when pushed shows air can be compressed (D).

diagram:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 440 200" width="440" font-family="Arial,sans-serif">
  <text x="220" y="16" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#1e293b">Changes of State in Water</text>
  <rect x="170" y="30" width="100" height="40" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="2" rx="6"/>
  <text x="220" y="55" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#1e40af">ICE (solid)</text>
  <rect x="170" y="120" width="100" height="40" fill="#bae6fd" stroke="#0ea5e9" stroke-width="2" rx="6"/>
  <text x="220" y="145" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#0369a1">WATER (liquid)</text>
  <rect x="20" y="75" width="110" height="40" fill="#e0f2fe" stroke="#7dd3fc" stroke-width="1.5" rx="6"/>
  <text x="75" y="100" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#0284c7">WATER VAPOUR</text>
  <path d="M170,50 L130,80" stroke="#f97316" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arrorange)"/>
  <text x="142" y="62" font-size="10" fill="#f97316" font-weight="bold">A gain heat</text>
  <path d="M220,70 L220,120" stroke="#f97316" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arrorange)"/>
  <text x="228" y="100" font-size="10" fill="#f97316">gain heat</text>
  <path d="M130,95 L170,115" stroke="#6366f1" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arrindigo)"/>
  <text x="125" y="112" font-size="10" fill="#6366f1">condense</text>
  <path d="M170,140 L135,100" stroke="#6366f1" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arrindigo)" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/>
  <text x="50" y="127" font-size="10" fill="#6366f1">C lose heat</text>
  <path d="M270,140 L330,140" stroke="#64748b" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="350" y="145" font-size="11" fill="#0369a1">D = water vapour</text>
  <text x="350" y="158" font-size="9" fill="#64748b">(evaporation, gain heat)</text>
  <defs>
    <marker id="arrorange" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="3" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#f97316"/></marker>
    <marker id="arrindigo" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="3" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#6366f1"/></marker>
  </defs>
  <text x="220" y="190" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">A: gain (melt) | B: lose (freeze) | C: lose (condense) | D: water vapour</text>
</svg>
template: {CHARACTER_0} studied a diagram showing changes of state in water. Process A: ice to water; Process B: water to ice; Process C: water vapour to water; Process D: water to water vapour. Which correctly describes processes A, B, C and D as gaining or losing heat?
options: A: lose heat / B: gain heat / C: lose heat / D: steam
A: gain heat / B: lose heat / C: gain heat / D: water vapour
A: lose heat / B: gain heat / C: gain heat / D: steam
A: gain heat / B: lose heat / C: lose heat / D: water vapour
correct_answer: A: gain heat / B: lose heat / C: lose heat / D: water vapour
explanation: Ice gains heat to melt into water (A = gain heat). Water loses heat to freeze (B = lose heat). Water vapour loses heat to condense (C = lose heat). Evaporation produces water vapour (D = water vapour).

  <rect x="155" y="50" width="100" height="40" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="2" rx="4"/>
  <text x="205" y="68" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#1e40af">LIQUID</text>
  <text x="205" y="82" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#64748b">(given: 25°C)</text>

template: {CHARACTER_0} studied substance W: it is a liquid at 25°C and becomes a gas at 60°C. Statement C says substance W is a solid at 5°C. Is this true, false, or not possible to tell?
explanation: We only know W is a liquid at 25°C and a gas at 60°C. Without knowing its freezing point, we cannot determine if it will be solid or liquid at 5°C.

<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 380 170" width="380" font-family="Arial,sans-serif">
  <text x="190" y="16" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#1e293b">Thermometer Strips: Metal vs. Plastic Tray</text>
  <rect x="20" y="25" width="340" height="26" fill="#334155" rx="3"/>

template: {CHARACTER_0} pasted thermometer strips on two trays — one metal and one plastic — and placed a hand on each for 10 seconds. Which data table correctly shows the temperature readings? (Metal conducts heat better than plastic.)
options: Metal tray temperature increases more; plastic tray stays same
Plastic tray increases more than metal tray
correct_answer: Metal tray temperature increases more; plastic tray stays same
explanation: Metal is a better conductor of heat than plastic. It conducts heat away from the hand faster, causing the metal tray's temperature to rise more quickly in the first 10 seconds.

template: {CHARACTER_0} used toy shadows to draw animals. To create shadows of animals of different sizes, which should be done? A: Trace shadows at different times of the day. B: Place the animal toys at different parts of the paper. C: Draw the shadow when it is cloudy.
explanation: To change shadow size, the distance between the toy and light source must change. Tracing at different times (A) changes the Sun's position, and moving the toys (B) changes where shadows fall.

template: {CHARACTER_0} studied a man bowling. Which best shows the energy conversions when the bowling ball rolls and hits the pins?
options: kinetic energy (ball) → heat energy (pins) → kinetic energy (pins)
potential energy (man) → kinetic energy (ball) → sound + heat energy (pins)
kinetic energy (ball) → kinetic energy (pins) + sound energy (pins) + heat energy (pins)
potential energy (ball) → potential energy (man) → kinetic energy (ball) → sound + heat energy (pins)
correct_answer: kinetic energy (ball) → kinetic energy (pins) + sound energy (pins) + heat energy (pins)
explanation: When the rolling ball hits the pins: its kinetic energy is transferred to the pins (kinetic energy), released as sound (crashing sound), and lost as heat energy due to friction and impact.

template: {CHARACTER_0} read about a coal-powered hair dryer. Energy moves from point X (coal stored) to point Y (electrical energy delivered). As energy moves from X to Y, the energy stored in the coal ___.
options: reduces the friction in the turbine generator
increases the sound energy in the hair dryer
explanation: At point X, the coal has chemical potential energy. This is burned to produce steam that turns a turbine and generator, converting that energy into electrical energy at point Y.

template: {CHARACTER_0} studied a circuit with four similar batteries, four similar bulbs, and four bars W, X, Y, Z made of different materials. Only one bulb is lit. Which classification of the 4 materials is correct?
options: W and X = conductors, Y and Z = insulators
W and Y = conductors, X and Z = insulators
Y and Z = conductors, W and X = insulators
correct_answer: Y and Z = conductors, W and X = insulators
explanation: Electricity must flow through conductors to light a bulb. If only one bulb is lit, it means Y and Z (in the lit bulb's circuit) are conductors while W and X are insulators.

template: {CHARACTER_0} studied four circuits with different arrangements of identical batteries and identical bulbs. All bulbs light up. Circuit E has most batteries and fewest bulbs (brightest); Circuit F has many batteries; Circuit G has fewest batteries and most bulbs (dimmest); Circuit H is in between. Which shows brightness low/medium/high?
options: low: G / medium: H / high: E
low: G / medium: E / high: F
low: E / medium: F / high: G
low: E / medium: H / high: F
correct_answer: low: G / medium: H / high: E
explanation: Circuit F has the most batteries and fewest bulbs — brightest (high). Circuit G has the most bulbs and fewest batteries — dimmest (low). Circuit H is medium brightness.

template: {CHARACTER_0} made a magnet holder with different numbers of tape layers between magnet and paper clips. Results: no tape = 19 clips; 1 tape = 11; 3 tapes = 7; 5 tapes = 5; 7 tapes = 3. What is being investigated?
options: How magnet strength is affected by magnetic materials around it
How non-magnetic material amount determines the size of a magnet
How gravity affects the magnetic force on paper clips
How the strength of a magnet is affected by the distance between paper clips and the magnet
correct_answer: How the strength of a magnet is affected by the distance between paper clips and the magnet
explanation: By adding layers of tape, the distance between the magnet and the paper clips increases. The results show that as distance increases, the magnetic force weakens and fewer clips are attracted.

  <text x="243" y="107" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#475569">between tyre</text>
  <text x="243" y="121" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#475569">and icy road</text>

template: {CHARACTER_0} read about snow chains fixed to car tyres in winter. What is the purpose of the snow chain?
options: To increase the mass of the car.
To increase friction on the slippery road.
To help the cars move in a straight path.
To increase the potential energy of the car.
correct_answer: To increase friction on the slippery road.
explanation: Snow and ice are very smooth and slippery. The metal chains create a rougher surface on the tyres to increase friction, helping the car grip the road so it doesn't skid.

template: {CHARACTER_0} studied a classification chart for animals X, Y and Z. Which row shows the correct classification?
explanation: A tortoise is a reptile (X) with scales and a shell. A fish (Y) has scales but no shell. A salamander (Z) is an amphibian with moist skin and no scales or shell.

template: {CHARACTER_0} studied a flow chart about four organisms P, Q, R and S. Which statement is correct?
explanation: In the flow chart, R breaks down dead matter, which is the role of a decomposer like bacteria. Organism R is correctly identified as bacteria.

template: {CHARACTER_0} studied the food chain: Grass → Goat → Lion. Which terms describe the goat?
explanation: The goat eats grass, making it a consumer. Because the lion hunts and eats the goat, the goat is also prey. The goat is not a predator as it does not hunt other animals.

template: {CHARACTER_0} compared the young of a cockroach (nymph) and a butterfly (caterpillar). Which similarities are correct? A: Both do not have wings. B: Both do not resemble their adults. C: Both have the same number of stages in their life cycle. D: Both moult several times to grow bigger.
explanation: Both the cockroach nymph and butterfly larva (caterpillar) do not have wings (A) and both moult several times as they grow (D). B is wrong — a nymph resembles its adult. C is wrong — they have different numbers of life stages.

template: {CHARACTER_0} observed a seedling over several days and plotted a graph. As the seedling grew, the Y-axis value decreased. What could the vertical axis Y represent?
options: Length of the root of the seedling.
Size of the seed leaves of the seedling.
correct_answer: Size of the seed leaves of the seedling.
explanation: As the seedling grows into an adult plant, its seed leaves (cotyledons) shrink and eventually disappear. This decreasing trend fits the graph.

template: {CHARACTER_0} studied the human reproductive system diagram with parts P, Q, R, S and T. Which correctly shows which part produces male reproductive cells, female reproductive cells, and where the fertilised egg develops into a baby?
options: P produces male / R produces female / T is where baby develops
P produces male / S produces female / T is where baby develops
Q produces male / R produces female / S is where baby develops
Q produces male / T produces female / S is where baby develops
correct_answer: Q produces male / R produces female / S is where baby develops
explanation: Part Q (testis) produces male reproductive cells (sperm). Part R (ovary) produces female reproductive cells (eggs). Part S (womb/uterus) is where a fertilised egg develops into a baby.

template: {CHARACTER_0} studied two flowers U and V from the same plant. Arrows P, Q, R and S show possible pollen grain transfers. Which arrows show the transfer of pollen grains during pollination?
explanation: Pollination is the transfer of pollen from the anther (male part) to the stigma (female part). Arrows Q and S show pollen moving from an anther to a stigma.

template: {CHARACTER_0} studied two plants A and B that grow on land. Plant B grows near the shore with fruits found in the sea. Which row shows the characteristics of the fruits of plants A and B?
options: A: fibrous husk / B: pod-like structure
A: fibrous husk / B: wing-like structure
correct_answer: A: fibrous husk / B: wing-like structure
explanation: Plant B's fruits are found in the sea, indicating they float using a fibrous husk (water dispersal). Plant A grows further from water and likely has fruits with wing-like structures for wind dispersal.

  <rect x="20" y="35" width="155" height="100" fill="#f8fafc" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="2" rx="6"/>
  <text x="98" y="52" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#475569">Set-up P (Liquid Y)</text>
  <text x="98" y="67" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#475569">Liquid Y absorbs O₂</text>
  <text x="98" y="81" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#475569">Seeds in no-O₂ water</text>
  <text x="98" y="100" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">Result: Seeds do NOT germinate</text>
  <rect x="205" y="35" width="155" height="100" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="2" rx="6"/>
  <text x="283" y="52" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#15803d">Set-up Q ★ (water)</text>
  <text x="283" y="67" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#15803d">Normal water (has O₂)</text>
  <text x="283" y="81" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#15803d">Seeds with O₂ access</text>
  <text x="283" y="100" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#15803d">Result: Seeds germinate ✓</text>
  <text x="190" y="155" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">Conclusion: oxygen is needed for germination</text>
  <text x="190" y="168" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">Set-up P shows: without O₂, seeds do not germinate</text>
</svg>
template: {CHARACTER_0} conducted a germination experiment with two set-ups. Set-up P used Liquid Y (absorbs oxygen); Set-up Q used water with dry cotton wool. What changes are needed to ensure a fair test? A: Add two more seeds to set-up Q. B: Change water in Q to liquid Y. C: Add water to the dry cotton wool in set-up Q.
explanation: To test if oxygen affects germination, only the presence of oxygen should differ. Adding seeds to Q (A) ensures equal seed numbers. Adding water to dry cotton wool in Q (C) ensures both have moisture. Only the absence of oxygen in P (liquid Y) is the variable.

template: {CHARACTER_0} jogged in the park. A diagram shows blood flow direction. Which graph shows the amount of oxygen in the blood at different parts of the body (A = returning to heart, B = just left lungs)?
options: A has most oxygen, B has least
B has most oxygen, A has least
Both A and B have equal oxygen
A and B alternate in oxygen level
correct_answer: B has most oxygen, A has least
explanation: Blood at B just left the lungs and has the most oxygen. Blood at A has returned from the body to the heart and is headed to the lungs, so it has the least oxygen.

  <rect x="135" y="70" width="50" height="85" fill="#e0f2fe" stroke="#7dd3fc" stroke-width="1.5" rx="4"/>

  <text x="160" y="169" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#64748b">Roots (absorb water)</text>
  <line x1="145" y1="155" x2="145" y2="75" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="2.5" marker-end="url(#arrPT)"/>
  <text x="120" y="115" font-size="9" fill="#3b82f6" text-anchor="middle">Water ↑</text>
  <text x="120" y="127" font-size="8" fill="#3b82f6" text-anchor="middle">(xylem)</text>

</svg>
template: {CHARACTER_0} studied a diagram showing how water and food move through a plant. Which diagram shows the correct movement of water and food to different parts of a plant?
options: Water moves in all directions; food moves downward only
Water moves up only; food moves both up and down
Water moves down only; food moves up only
Both water and food move in one direction only
correct_answer: Water moves up only; food moves both up and down
explanation: Water is taken up from roots and travels upward only through the stem to leaves and fruits. Food is made in leaves and travels both upward and downward through the stem to all parts of the plant.

template: {CHARACTER_0} studied different types of beaks and feet of birds. Which row shows the beak, feet and diet of a bird in a specific habitat? Beak A: long thin. Beak B: short strong. Beak C: flat wide. Foot E: webbed. Foot F: perching.
options: aquatic habitat / beak C / foot D / diet: nuts
aquatic habitat / beak B / foot F / diet: fish
land habitat / beak A / foot F / diet: nectar
land habitat / beak B / foot E / diet: grains
correct_answer: land habitat / beak A / foot F / diet: nectar
explanation: A land bird that eats nectar needs a long, thin beak (A) to reach into flowers and perching feet (F) suitable for resting on branches.

template: {CHARACTER_0} studied a habitat diagram showing dragonflies, water hyacinth, fish, cattail and Amazon sword plants. (Dragonfly nymph is the same population as the dragonfly.) Which statement is correct?
options: There are eight populations of producers.
There are three populations of consumers.
There is one community with five populations.
There is one community with six populations.
correct_answer: There is one community with five populations.
explanation: A community is all different populations living together in one habitat. There are five distinct populations: dragonfly, water hyacinth, fish, cattail and Amazon sword (dragonfly nymph is the same population as dragonfly).

diagram:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 420 130" width="420" font-family="Arial,sans-serif">
  <text x="210" y="16" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#1e293b">Murky River — Order of Events</text>
  <defs><marker id="arrRV" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="3" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#475569"/></marker></defs>
  <rect x="5" y="40" width="90" height="50" fill="#bae6fd" stroke="#0ea5e9" stroke-width="1.5" rx="4"/>
  <text x="50" y="58" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" font-weight="bold" fill="#0369a1">Heavy rain</text>
  <text x="50" y="72" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8" fill="#0369a1">washes soil</text>
  <text x="50" y="84" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8" fill="#0369a1">into river</text>
  <line x1="95" y1="65" x2="110" y2="65" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#arrRV)"/>
  <rect x="111" y="40" width="78" height="50" fill="#fef3c7" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="1.5" rx="4"/>
  <text x="150" y="58" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" font-weight="bold" fill="#92400e">D</text>
  <text x="150" y="70" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8" fill="#92400e">Murky water</text>
  <text x="150" y="82" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8" fill="#92400e">(less sunlight)</text>
  <line x1="189" y1="65" x2="204" y2="65" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#arrRV)"/>
  <rect x="205" y="40" width="78" height="50" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#f87171" stroke-width="1.5" rx="4"/>
  <text x="244" y="58" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" font-weight="bold" fill="#dc2626">A</text>
  <text x="244" y="70" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8" fill="#dc2626">Plants decrease</text>
  <text x="244" y="82" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8" fill="#dc2626">(less food/O₂)</text>
  <line x1="283" y1="65" x2="298" y2="65" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#arrRV)"/>
  <rect x="299" y="40" width="78" height="50" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#f87171" stroke-width="1.5" rx="4"/>
  <text x="338" y="58" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" font-weight="bold" fill="#dc2626">B</text>
  <text x="338" y="70" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8" fill="#dc2626">Fish decrease</text>
  <text x="338" y="82" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8" fill="#dc2626">(less food/O₂)</text>
  <text x="210" y="112" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">Correct order: D → A → B (then C: algae blooms later)</text>
</svg>
template: {CHARACTER_0} studied events when soil washed into a river making the water murky. A: Plant population decreased. B: Fish population decreased. C: Plants trapped less light. D: Fish had less oxygen. What is the correct order of events?
explanation: First, murky water causes plants to trap less light (C). This leads to a decrease in plant population (A). With fewer plants doing photosynthesis, there is less oxygen in the water (D), eventually causing fish population to decrease (B).

diagram:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 380 150" width="380" font-family="Arial,sans-serif">
  <text x="190" y="16" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#1e293b">Time to Cool from 80°C to Room Temp</text>
  <rect x="30" y="24" width="320" height="26" fill="#334155" rx="3"/>
  <text x="100" y="41" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="white" text-anchor="middle">Container</text>

  <line x1="170" y1="24" x2="170" y2="140" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/>
  <rect x="30" y="50" width="320" height="22" fill="#f8fafc" stroke="#e2e8f0" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="100" y="65" font-size="11" fill="#1e293b" text-anchor="middle">W</text><text x="255" y="65" font-size="11" fill="#1e293b" text-anchor="middle">38</text>
  <rect x="30" y="72" width="320" height="22" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#e2e8f0" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="100" y="87" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626" text-anchor="middle">X (fastest)</text><text x="255" y="87" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626" text-anchor="middle">21 (best conductor)</text>
  <rect x="30" y="94" width="320" height="22" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="100" y="109" font-size="11" fill="#15803d" font-weight="bold" text-anchor="middle">Y ★ (slowest)</text><text x="255" y="109" font-size="11" fill="#15803d" font-weight="bold" text-anchor="middle">63 (poorest conductor)</text>
  <rect x="30" y="116" width="320" height="22" fill="#f8fafc" stroke="#e2e8f0" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="100" y="131" font-size="11" fill="#1e293b" text-anchor="middle">Z</text><text x="255" y="131" font-size="11" fill="#1e293b" text-anchor="middle">47</text>
  <rect x="30" y="24" width="320" height="114" fill="none" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1.5" rx="3"/>
  <text x="190" y="148" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#15803d">Y is the poorest conductor → best for keeping beverages cold</text>
</svg>
template: {CHARACTER_0} poured hot water at 80°C into four identical containers W, X, Y and Z made of different materials. Time to cool to room temperature: W=38 min, X=21 min, Y=63 min, Z=47 min. Which container is most suitable to keep a beverage cold the longest?
explanation: Container Y took the longest time to cool (63 min), meaning it is the poorest conductor of heat. A poor heat conductor is best for keeping cold beverages cold because it slows down heat gain from warmer surroundings.

diagram:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 420 175" width="420" font-family="Arial,sans-serif">
  <text x="210" y="16" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#1e293b">States of Substances at 30°C</text>
  <rect x="20" y="24" width="380" height="26" fill="#334155" rx="3"/>
  <text x="65" y="41" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="white" text-anchor="middle">Substance</text>
  <text x="185" y="41" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="white" text-anchor="middle">Freeze (°C)</text>
  <text x="295" y="41" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="white" text-anchor="middle">Boil (°C)</text>
  <text x="385" y="41" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="white" text-anchor="middle">At 30°C</text>
  <line x1="120" y1="24" x2="120" y2="148" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="245" y1="24" x2="245" y2="148" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="345" y1="24" x2="345" y2="148" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/>
  <rect x="20" y="50" width="380" height="28" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#e2e8f0" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="65" y="68" font-size="11" fill="#1e293b" text-anchor="middle">A</text><text x="185" y="68" font-size="11" fill="#1e293b" text-anchor="middle">20</text><text x="295" y="68" font-size="11" fill="#1e293b" text-anchor="middle">85</text><text x="385" y="68" font-size="11" fill="#0284c7" font-weight="bold" text-anchor="middle">liquid</text>
  <rect x="20" y="78" width="380" height="28" fill="#e0e7ff" stroke="#6366f1" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="65" y="96" font-size="11" fill="#3730a3" font-weight="bold" text-anchor="middle">B ★</text><text x="185" y="96" font-size="11" fill="#3730a3" font-weight="bold" text-anchor="middle">35</text><text x="295" y="96" font-size="11" fill="#1e293b" text-anchor="middle">110</text><text x="385" y="96" font-size="11" fill="#3730a3" font-weight="bold" text-anchor="middle">SOLID</text>
  <rect x="20" y="106" width="380" height="28" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#e2e8f0" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="65" y="124" font-size="11" fill="#1e293b" text-anchor="middle">C</text><text x="185" y="124" font-size="11" fill="#1e293b" text-anchor="middle">15</text><text x="295" y="124" font-size="11" fill="#1e293b" text-anchor="middle">60</text><text x="385" y="124" font-size="11" fill="#0284c7" font-weight="bold" text-anchor="middle">liquid</text>
  <rect x="20" y="24" width="380" height="110" fill="none" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1.5" rx="3"/>
  <text x="210" y="162" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#3730a3">B is solid at 30°C because 30°C is below its freezing point (35°C)</text>
</svg>
template: {CHARACTER_0} studied freezing and boiling points: Substance A (freeze 20°C, boil 85°C), B (freeze 35°C, boil 110°C), C (freeze 15°C, boil 60°C). What are the states of A, B and C at 30°C?
options: A: liquid / B: solid / C: liquid
A: gas / B: solid / C: liquid
A: liquid / B: liquid / C: gas
A: solid / B: liquid / C: liquid
correct_answer: A: liquid / B: solid / C: liquid
explanation: At 30°C, A is liquid (between 20°C freeze and 85°C boil). B is solid (30°C is below its 35°C freezing point). C is liquid (between 15°C freeze and 60°C boil).

diagram:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 300 170" width="300" font-family="Arial,sans-serif">
  <text x="150" y="16" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#1e293b">Measuring Volume of Irregular Glass Bottle</text>

template: {CHARACTER_0} wanted to find the volume of an irregular glass bottle. Which apparatus should be used?
explanation: To find the volume of an irregular container, fill it to the brim with water, then pour that water into a measuring cylinder to read the exact volume.

template: {CHARACTER_0} placed two identical bowls A and B with 12g of liquid P. Bowl A: 30°C, 60 cm² exposed area → 8g remaining. Bowl B: 30°C but temperature T, 60 cm² → 10g remaining. Bowl C: temperature 30°C, S cm² area → 7g remaining. Bowl D: 30°C, 50 cm² → 9g remaining. What are T and S?
explanation: Bowl B had more liquid left (10g vs 8g), so evaporation was slower at lower temperature T=40°C. Bowl C had less liquid left (7g vs 9g), so evaporation was faster with larger surface area S=40 cm².

diagram:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 380 160" width="380" font-family="Arial,sans-serif">
  <text x="190" y="16" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#1e293b">Electric Iron — Parts and Materials</text>
  <rect x="20" y="30" width="155" height="90" fill="#fef3c7" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="2" rx="6"/>
  <text x="98" y="48" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#92400e">Part P — Base plate</text>
  <text x="98" y="63" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#475569">Conducts heat quickly</text>
  <text x="98" y="78" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#475569">→ Must be METAL</text>
  <text x="98" y="93" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#64748b">(e.g. stainless steel)</text>
  <text x="98" y="110" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#f59e0b">Good heat conductor</text>
  <rect x="205" y="30" width="155" height="90" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="2" rx="6"/>
  <text x="283" y="48" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#1e40af">Part Q — Wire cover</text>
  <text x="283" y="63" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#475569">Prevents electric shock</text>
  <text x="283" y="78" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#475569">→ Must be PLASTIC/RUBBER</text>
  <text x="283" y="93" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#64748b">(insulator)</text>
  <text x="283" y="110" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#3b82f6">Good electrical insulator</text>
  <text x="190" y="148" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">P = metal (heat conduction); Q outer cover = plastic (electrical insulation)</text>
</svg>
template: {CHARACTER_0} studied an electric iron. Part P is the base (must conduct heat fast); the outer covering of Part Q is the wire (must be an electrical insulator). The flowchart shows materials A (insulator), B, C (good heat conductor), D. Which pair is suitable for Part P and outer covering of Part Q?
options: Part P: D / Part Q: B
Part P: C / Part Q: A
Part P: C / Part Q: B
Part P: D / Part Q: A
correct_answer: Part P: C / Part Q: A
explanation: Part P (iron base) must conduct heat fast → Material C. The outer covering of Part Q (wire) must be an electrical insulator to prevent electric shock → Material A.

template: {CHARACTER_0} placed a paper spiral above a circuit. When the switch was closed, the wire heated up and the spiral began to spin. Which correctly describes the energy conversion from when the switch was closed until the spiral started spinning?
options: electrical energy → heat + light energy → kinetic energy
potential energy → heat energy → kinetic energy + sound energy
kinetic energy → electrical energy → heat energy → kinetic energy
potential energy → electrical energy → heat energy → kinetic energy
correct_answer: electrical energy → heat + light energy → kinetic energy
explanation: Electrical energy from the circuit is converted to heat and light energy in the wire. The heat warms the air, which rises and provides kinetic energy to make the paper spiral spin.

template: {CHARACTER_0} read that a bakery uses a food display warmer with two identical bulbs. The bulbs give more heat when brighter. Which circuit should be used so the bulbs give out the most heat?
options: Parallel circuit (Circuit 4) with 2 batteries and both bulbs in parallel
correct_answer: Parallel circuit (Circuit 4) with 2 batteries and both bulbs in parallel
explanation: To get the most heat, the bulbs must be as bright as possible. In a parallel circuit (Circuit 4), each bulb receives the full voltage of the batteries, making them brighter than in series.

template: {CHARACTER_0} wanted to check if aquarium water was at the right temperature. Which diagram shows the correct way to measure the temperature?
options: Bulb touching the bottom of the aquarium
Bulb fully submerged, not touching sides or bottom
Bulb only partially submerged at water surface
correct_answer: Bulb fully submerged, not touching sides or bottom
explanation: For an accurate reading, the thermometer bulb must be fully submerged in the water without touching the sides or bottom, which would measure the glass temperature instead.

template: {CHARACTER_0} placed two empty identical beakers J and K at room temperature. Beaker J got 200 cm³ of boiling water; beaker K got 400 cm³. Temperatures were recorded every minute. Which graph correctly shows the results?
options: J and K start and end at the same temperatures throughout
Both start at 100°C; K stays higher longer; both cool to room temperature
Both start at 100°C; J stays higher longer; both cool to room temperature
correct_answer: Both start at 100°C; K stays higher longer; both cool to room temperature
explanation: Both beakers start at 100°C. Beaker K has more water (400 cm³) so it has more heat energy and loses heat more slowly, staying higher in temperature than J. Eventually both cool to room temperature.

template: {CHARACTER_0} attached a strong magnet to a spring at the top of a ramp with lubricant on the surface. A steel coin at the bottom moved up the ramp. What forces acted on the steel coin as it moved up? A: Magnetic force. B: Frictional force. C: Gravitational force. D: Elastic spring force.
explanation: Magnetic force pulls the coin up the ramp (A). Frictional force acts between coin and ramp (B). Gravitational force acts on the coin pulling it downward (C). Elastic spring force acts on the magnet, not the coin.

  <text x="195" y="160" font-size="8" fill="#64748b">Low PE</text>
  <text x="195" y="170" font-size="8" fill="#64748b">Max KE</text>
  <text x="283" y="90" font-size="8" fill="#64748b">High PE</text>
  <text x="283" y="100" font-size="8" fill="#64748b">Zero KE</text>
  <text x="190" y="190" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">L/N (top): max PE, zero KE; M (bottom): max KE, min PE</text>
</svg>
template: {CHARACTER_0} studied a ball hanging from point K, pulled to point L and released. It swung to point M (lowest) then up to point N. Which graph correctly shows kinetic energy (KE) and potential energy (PE) as the ball moved from M to N?
explanation: As the ball moves from M (lowest point) to N (highest point), it gains height so PE increases. It slows down as it rises, so KE decreases.

diagram:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 400 180" width="400" font-family="Arial,sans-serif">
  <text x="200" y="16" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#1e293b">Bar Magnet Arrangements</text>
  <rect x="20" y="35" width="170" height="60" fill="#f8fafc" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1.5" rx="4"/>
  <text x="105" y="50" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#475569">Repulsion (like poles)</text>
  <rect x="30" y="56" width="55" height="24" fill="#ef4444" stroke="#b91c1c" stroke-width="1.5" rx="2"/>
  <text x="44" y="72" font-size="10" fill="white" font-weight="bold">N</text>
  <text x="74" y="72" font-size="10" fill="#fde68a" font-weight="bold">S</text>
  <rect x="105" y="56" width="55" height="24" fill="#3b82f6" stroke="#1d4ed8" stroke-width="1.5" rx="2"/>
  <text x="119" y="72" font-size="10" fill="#fde68a" font-weight="bold">S</text>
  <text x="149" y="72" font-size="10" fill="white" font-weight="bold">N</text>
  <text x="83" y="72" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626" font-weight="bold">↔</text>
  <text x="105" y="87" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#dc2626">S faces S → repel</text>
  <rect x="210" y="35" width="170" height="60" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a" stroke-width="2" rx="4"/>
  <text x="295" y="50" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#15803d">Attraction ★ (unlike)</text>
  <rect x="220" y="56" width="55" height="24" fill="#ef4444" stroke="#b91c1c" stroke-width="1.5" rx="2"/>
  <text x="234" y="72" font-size="10" fill="white" font-weight="bold">N</text>
  <text x="264" y="72" font-size="10" fill="#fde68a" font-weight="bold">S</text>
  <rect x="295" y="56" width="55" height="24" fill="#ef4444" stroke="#b91c1c" stroke-width="1.5" rx="2"/>
  <text x="309" y="72" font-size="10" fill="white" font-weight="bold">N</text>
  <text x="339" y="72" font-size="10" fill="#fde68a" font-weight="bold">S</text>
  <text x="273" y="72" font-size="11" fill="#15803d" font-weight="bold">→←</text>
  <text x="295" y="87" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#15803d">S faces N → attract</text>
  <rect x="20" y="110" width="360" height="30" fill="#fef9c3" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="1.5" rx="4"/>
  <text x="200" y="129" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#92400e">Rule: Unlike poles (N↔S) attract; Like poles (N↔N or S↔S) repel</text>

</svg>
template: {CHARACTER_0} studied six bar magnets A to L arranged in a diagram. Which arrangement using four of the magnets is possible based on the poles shown?
explanation: In magnet arrangements, unlike poles attract and like poles repel. Based on the original diagram, arrangement (4) is possible because the poles at the touching ends are unlike and thus attract each other.
